walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Hex Map
walkthrough.text: Welcome to the arms race... except everyone is a farm animal. Your pigs have nukes. Your chickens have spies. This is going to get weird. First, grab all the hex territory tiles and lay them out to form the map. Each hex shows a different terrain type. You will see lush farmlands, smoky factories, dense forests, rocky quarries, and golden wheat fields. Arrange them in the pattern shown in the rulebook. This is your Cold War battlefield, and every hex is worth fighting over.
walkthrough.image_caption: Hex territory tiles showing farms, factories, forests, quarries, and wheat fields

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Faction
walkthrough.text: Each player picks one of the four factions. You have got the Cows, led by a bespectacled rocket scientist. The Chickens, a scrappy guerrilla fighter with a bandolier. The Pigs, a decorated military general puffing a cigar. And the Sheep, a cold-eyed rifle-toting operative. Each faction card has gorgeous Cold War propaganda art. Pick your favorite animal warlord and take the matching faction card.
walkthrough.image_caption: Four faction cards — Chickens, Pigs, Cows, and Sheep

walkthrough.title: Take Your Meeples and Buildings
walkthrough.text: Now grab all the wooden pieces in your faction's color. You get a pile of animal-shaped meeples representing your herds. These adorable little wooden farm animals are about to do terrible things to each other. You also get building pieces: small house shapes and larger barn shapes. The pigs, for example, get pink pig meeples and pink buildings. Keep everything in front of you for now.
walkthrough.image_caption: Pink pig meeples with house and barn building pieces

walkthrough.title: Sort the Resource Cards
walkthrough.text: Find all the resource cards and separate them into five supply piles by type. You will see Wood, showing stacked logs in a blue-bordered card. Wheat, showing a sack of grain in a yellow-bordered card. Brick, showing a wheelbarrow of bricks in a red-bordered card. Weapons, showing a mortar launcher in a green-bordered card. And Plutonium, showing a centrifuge in a gray-bordered card. Yes, your farm animals have access to weapons-grade nuclear material. Place all five piles where everyone can reach them.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five resource card types — Brick, Wheat, Wood, Plutonium, and Weapons

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Token Supply
walkthrough.text: Now organize the tokens into three supply piles. First, the nuke tokens. These are round tokens with a mushroom cloud explosion on a green background. Stack them up. They represent your weapons of mass destruction. Second, the VP tokens. These are round tokens showing a sheriff star badge on a red-orange background. These track your victory points. Third, the turmoil tokens. These are round tokens with a dark blue building-on-fire icon. These mark regions that have been devastated by nukes or conflict.
walkthrough.image_caption: Nuke tokens showing mushroom cloud explosions

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Dice
walkthrough.text: There are two types of dice in Farms Race, and they could not be more different. The white resource dice are used during the Resources phase to determine what you collect. Each face shows a different resource icon: brick, wheat, wood, weapons, or plutonium. The red combat dice are used when your herds get into fights. Their faces show guns, spy silhouettes with fedora hats, and animal head icons. Separate them into two pools so you do not mix them up.
walkthrough.image_caption: White resource dice showing brick, wheat, plutonium, and wood faces

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Espionage Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the eleven espionage cards. These are the action cards your spies can play for devastating effects. You will see cards like Blackmail, Propaganda, Double Agent, Hacking, Thievery, Pocket Nuke, and Field Hospital, plus special VP-granting cards like Capitalism, Carnivorous, Domestication, and Plumbing. Each one has hilariously illustrated Cold War farm animal art. Shuffle them face-down into a draw pile.
walkthrough.image_caption: All eleven espionage cards spread out showing their illustrations

walkthrough.title: Draw Mutation Cards
walkthrough.text: Now for the really weird part. Shuffle the eleven mutation cards face-down. Each player draws one mutation card and keeps it. This is your faction's unique genetic anomaly for the entire game. Mutations include things like Flying, Parasitic, Hyper-Intelligent, Radioactive, Adorable, Zombie, Insecure, Deranged, Regenerative, Lucky, and Blue-Blooded. Each one fundamentally changes how your faction plays. The card back shows a DNA helix with the Farms Race logo. Do not show your mutation to other players until you use it.
walkthrough.image_caption: All eleven mutation cards showing unique powers and farm animal art

walkthrough.title: Distribute Spy Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a set of spy tokens in their faction color. These are round tokens numbered 1, 2, and 3, plus one with a spy crosshair icon. You will use these to place secret agents on the board during the game. Keep them hidden from opponents. Espionage is a core mechanic, because nothing says Cold War like farm animals spying on each other.
walkthrough.image_caption: Spy tokens numbered 1, 2, 3 plus spy crosshair token

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Herds
walkthrough.text: Following the setup rules for your player count, each player places their starting herds on the hex map. You will typically place a few herd meeples on territories near your starting corner. This is your initial foothold in the great Farms Race. Choose wisely, because early positioning determines your resource access and expansion options.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Award Cards and Reference
walkthrough.text: Place the award cards face-up where everyone can see them. The Nukiest award, for example, goes to whoever builds the most nukes and is worth 1 VP. It shows a chicken riding a nuclear missile like a rodeo cowboy. Finally, place the reference cards showing Building Costs and Turn Order where all players can see them. These are your cheat sheets for the game. The reference card shows all four phases and all building costs at a glance.
walkthrough.image_caption: Nukiest award card — 1 VP for the player who builds the most nukes

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: The youngest player goes first. Or if your group prefers, pick randomly. Play proceeds clockwise. Your farm animals are armed, mutated, and ready for war. Let the Farms Race begin.

walkthrough.title: The Four-Phase Turn
walkthrough.text: Every turn in Farms Race follows a strict four-phase structure. Think of it like a military operation, because that is basically what it is, except your soldiers are livestock. Phase 1 is Preparation, Phase 2 is Actions, Phase 3 is Resources, and Phase 4 is Discard. You must complete them in order. The reference card has the full breakdown, so keep it handy.
walkthrough.image_caption: Reference card showing Turn Order and Building Costs

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Preparation
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, two things happen during Preparation. First, remove any turmoil tokens from regions you control. Those burning buildings get cleaned up as your faction rebuilds. Second, you gain new herds. Take fresh animal meeples from your supply and add them to your territories. This is how your army regenerates. Even in a nuclear wasteland, farm animals breed fast.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Actions
walkthrough.text: This is where the fun happens. During the Actions phase, you may move or heal each of your herds. Moving means shifting a herd meeple from one hex to an adjacent hex. Healing means restoring a damaged herd to full strength instead of moving it. You resolve each herd one at a time. If a herd moves into a hex occupied by an enemy, combat happens immediately. More on that in a moment.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Resources
walkthrough.text: Time to roll the resource dice. Take the two white dice and roll them. Each die shows one of five resource icons: brick, wheat, wood, weapons, or plutonium. Whatever you roll, you collect matching resource cards from the supply piles. Rolling two different resources gives you one of each. Rolling doubles gives you two of that resource. These cards are your currency for building things that will terrify your neighbors.
walkthrough.image_caption: White resource dice showing resource type icons

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Discard
walkthrough.text: If you have more than seven resource cards in your hand after collecting, you must discard down to seven. Choose which cards to discard. This hand limit forces tough decisions. Do you keep the plutonium for a future nuke, or the wood and wheat for a spy? Resource management is life-or-death in the Farms Race. Well, for your adorable wooden farm animal meeples, anyway.

walkthrough.title: Building Overview
walkthrough.text: Here is what makes Farms Race tick. You can build during phases 2, 3, or 4 of your turn. Check your reference card for costs. There are four things you can build. Houses cost 1 wood, 1 plutonium, 1 brick, and 1 wheat, and they are worth 1 VP. Barns cost 3 weapons, 1 brick, and 1 plutonium, and they are worth 2 VP. Nukes cost 1 weapon, 1 brick, and 1 plutonium. And Spies cost 1 wood and 1 wheat. Pay the resource cards, place the building or token, and watch your opponents sweat.
walkthrough.image_caption: Building Costs reference — House, Barn, Nuke, and Spy

walkthrough.title: Building: Houses and Barns
walkthrough.text: Houses and barns are your main source of victory points. A house is the cheaper option at 1 VP. It costs one of each basic resource: wood, plutonium, brick, and wheat. A barn is the heavy investment at 2 VP, costing three weapons, a brick, and plutonium. You place buildings on territories you control. Buildings stay even if you lose the territory, but you only score them if you control the region at game end. Protect your investments.

walkthrough.title: Building: Nukes
walkthrough.text: Ah, nukes. The reason this game exists. Building a nuke costs 1 weapon, 1 brick, and 1 plutonium. When you build one, you take a nuke token from the supply. That mushroom cloud token sitting in front of you is now a constant threat to everyone at the table. You do not have to use it right away. In fact, the threat of a nuke is often more powerful than actually detonating it. But when you do use it, it devastates a region and places a turmoil token there.
walkthrough.image_caption: Nuke tokens — mushroom cloud markers of mass destruction

walkthrough.title: Building: Spies
walkthrough.text: Spies cost just 1 wood and 1 wheat, making them the cheapest thing you can build. When you deploy a spy, place one of your numbered spy tokens on the board. Spies enable espionage actions and card plays. They are the gateway to some of the most disruptive plays in the game. A well-placed spy can steal resources, sabotage nukes, or spread propaganda. Never underestimate the intelligence community, especially when it is run by sheep.
walkthrough.image_caption: Numbered spy tokens — your covert agents

walkthrough.title: Combat
walkthrough.text: When one of your herds moves into a hex with enemy herds, combat breaks out. Both players roll the red combat dice. These dice show three types of faces: guns for direct damage, spy silhouettes for espionage effects, and animal heads for faction-specific abilities. Compare results to determine the outcome. Defeated herds are removed from the board. The victor holds the territory. Combat is fast, brutal, and surprisingly common when four militarized farm animals share a tiny hex map.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red combat dice with guns, spy hats, and animal head faces

walkthrough.title: Nuking a Region
walkthrough.text: To launch a nuke, spend one of your nuke tokens and choose a target region. Place a turmoil token on that region. All herds in the nuked region take damage. Buildings survive but the territory becomes contested wasteland until someone cleans up during their Preparation phase. The player who has built the most nukes over the course of the game earns the Nukiest award card, worth 1 VP. Mutually assured destruction has never been this cute.
walkthrough.image_caption: Turmoil tokens — placed on nuked or devastated regions

walkthrough.title: Espionage Cards
walkthrough.text: Espionage cards are powerful action cards you can play when you have spies deployed. There are eleven types. Blackmail lets you peek at an opponent's resources and steal up to 3. Field Hospital heals all your herds. Propaganda forces each opponent to give you a named resource. Double Agent steals 2 random resources from players with more VP. Hacking blocks an incoming nuke. Thievery takes 3 of one resource from the supply. And Pocket Nuke deals 2 damage without needing a nuke token. Four cards also award VP: Capitalism, Carnivorous, Domestication, and Plumbing, each worth 1 VP.
walkthrough.image_caption: All eleven espionage cards with their effects

walkthrough.title: Mutations
walkthrough.text: Each player's secret mutation card gives their faction a unique, game-changing power. Flying lets your herds move further. Parasitic lets you steal resources from adjacent players. Hyper-Intelligent gives extra resource production. Radioactive makes you immune to nuke damage. Adorable prevents opponents from attacking your regions under certain conditions. Zombie lets you raise defeated herds. Insecure allows special trade agreements. Deranged gives you chaotic resource abilities. Regenerative heals your herds in combat. Lucky lets you choose dice results instead of rolling. And Blue-Blooded gives you bonus starting resources. Each mutation creates a completely different play experience.
walkthrough.image_caption: All eleven mutation cards with unique faction powers

walkthrough.title: Award Cards
walkthrough.text: Award cards are face-up scoring bonuses that go to the player who best meets their condition. The Nukiest award goes to whoever has built the most nukes, worth 1 VP. There are additional award cards that reward different play styles, encouraging players to diversify their strategies. Awards are checked at game end and can swing close games. That extra VP from being the most aggressively nuclear can make all the difference.
walkthrough.image_caption: Nukiest award card — chicken riding a nuclear missile

walkthrough.title: Victory and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when a player reaches the VP threshold or when all territories on the hex map are controlled. At that point, add up your score. Count VP from houses, barns, award cards, and any territory control bonuses. Highest total wins. In case of a tie, the player with the most controlled territories wins. If still tied, the player with the most remaining herds breaks it. May the best-armed farm animal win.
walkthrough.image_caption: VP tokens — sheriff star badges for tracking victory points

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first round of Farms Race! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with four players, each commanding a different faction. Player 1 is the Cows, the yellow faction. Player 2 is the Chickens, the green faction. Player 3 is the Sheep, the gray faction. And Player 4 is the Pigs, the pink faction. Player 4 is going to make some very educational mistakes. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table.
walkthrough.image_caption: Four faction cards — one per player

walkthrough.title: The Round — Four Phases
walkthrough.text: Every round in Farms Race has four phases, always in this order: Preparation, Actions, Resources, Discard. Think of it as the military planning cycle for your absurdly armed livestock. Preparation gets your troops ready. Actions let you move, fight, and build. Resources give you the cards you need to build more terrible things. And Discard makes sure nobody hoards an entire arms depot in their hand. Let's walk through each phase with our four players.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1 — Preparation
walkthrough.text: It is the start of the round, so everyone runs their Preparation phase. Each player removes any turmoil tokens from regions they control — there are none yet, since nobody has been nuked. Then each player gains new herd meeples from their supply and places them on their territories. Fresh reinforcements reporting for duty. Your farm animals breed fast, which is convenient when they also have access to nuclear weapons.
walkthrough.image_caption: Herd meeples ready for deployment

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Cows) — Moves and Builds a House
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is playing it smart. During the Actions phase, the Cows move one herd into an adjacent territory to expand their footprint, then immediately build a house on their starting territory. That costs one wood, one plutonium, one brick, and one wheat — four different resources. But now Player 1 has 1 VP on the board. While everyone else is still plotting, the Cows already have points. That is how you win the Farms Race: build early, build often.
walkthrough.image_caption: Building Costs reference — House costs Wood + Plutonium + Brick + Wheat

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Chickens) — Resource Hoarder with a Plan
walkthrough.text: Player 2 takes a more cautious approach. The Chickens move their herds defensively, keeping them close together on resource-producing territories. Player 2 is collecting resources and waiting for the right moment to build. They also deploy a spy — that only costs one wood and one wheat, the cheapest build in the game. With a spy on the board, Player 2 now has access to espionage cards. The Chickens are playing the long game.
walkthrough.image_caption: Spy token deployed — enabling espionage card plays

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Sheep) — Aggressive Expansion
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is not here to make friends. The Sheep push their herds aggressively into territories bordering other players. One herd moves into a hex adjacent to Player 4's territory, staring down the Pigs across the border. Player 3 is not building yet — they are positioning for combat and territory grabs. This is a valid strategy, but it works best when you back it up with buildings. Territory without VP is just real estate.
walkthrough.image_caption: Hex map — Sheep herds pushing toward opponent territory

walkthrough.title: Player 4 (Pigs) — Mistake 1: Overextends Without Building
walkthrough.text: Player 4 surveys the map and gets excited. The Pigs move ALL their herds outward into new territories, spreading across the board like pink paint. They now control five hexes. Impressive, right? Wrong. Player 4 has zero buildings, zero VP, and herds stretched so thin that any single territory could be taken by a concentrated attack. Your pig general surveys the battlefield — technically an empty wheat field, but it is YOUR empty wheat field. For now.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Territory Is Just Potential
walkthrough.text: Territory is just potential — you need buildings to score VP. Do not spread out without building. A house on one territory is worth more than three empty territories. Player 1 has fewer hexes but already has 1 VP on the board. Player 4 has a sprawling empire of nothing. In Farms Race, the scoreboard does not care how much land you own. It cares how many houses and barns you built on it.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Cows) — Steady Expansion
walkthrough.text: Player 1 continues the balanced approach. The Cows move another herd into a new territory and collect resources from the dice roll. They are already eyeing their next building. With one house on the board and a growing resource hand, Player 1 is in excellent shape. This is what a well-paced game looks like: expand a little, build a little, repeat.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Chickens) — Plays a Spy Card Defensively
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws an espionage card — Hacking. This is the card that blocks an incoming nuke. Instead of using it immediately, the Chickens hold it in reserve. With Player 3 playing aggressively and nukes being built around the table, having Hacking in hand is like having a diplomatic immunity card. Player 2 is not flashy, but they are prepared. Smart chickens survive the longest.
walkthrough.image_caption: Espionage cards — Hacking blocks an incoming nuke

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Sheep) — Combat and Territory Grab
walkthrough.text: Player 3 pushes a herd into one of Player 4's thinly defended territories. Combat breaks out. Both players roll the red combat dice. Player 3 rolls well — guns and an animal head for a faction bonus. Player 4's single herd does not stand a chance. The Sheep take the territory. This is what happens when you overextend: your opponent picks off your outlying herds one by one. Player 4 just learned that lesson the hard way.
walkthrough.image_caption: Red combat dice — guns, spy hats, and animal heads

walkthrough.title: Player 4 (Pigs) — Mistake 2: Hoards Plutonium for a Nuke
walkthrough.text: Player 4 is frustrated after losing that territory. Instead of building a house or recovering their position, the Pigs decide to save up for a nuke. They hoard plutonium, brick, and weapons across multiple turns, watching other players build houses and rack up VP. By the time Player 4 finally builds the nuke and launches it at Player 3's territory, the damage is done — to Player 4's own game. The other three players have VP on the board. Player 4 has zero.
walkthrough.image_caption: Nuke token — flashy, but it does not score YOU points

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Nukes Don't Score You Points
walkthrough.text: Nukes are flashy but they do not score YOU points — they just hurt opponents. Build your own VP first before worrying about destroying others. You cannot win with a scoreboard of zero. Player 4 spent three turns worth of resources on a weapon that destroyed one enemy herd, while Players 1 and 2 spent those same turns building houses worth actual victory points. Build first, nuke later.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3 — Rolling for Resources
walkthrough.text: Let's watch the resource phase in action. Each player rolls the two white resource dice. Player 1 rolls wood and wheat — perfect for building another spy or contributing to a house. Player 2 rolls plutonium and brick — halfway to a nuke or a house. Player 3 rolls weapons and weapons — doubles, so they collect two weapons cards. That is progress toward a barn. Player 4 rolls wheat and wood. Useful resources, if Player 4 would actually build something with them.
walkthrough.image_caption: White resource dice — each face shows a different resource type

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Cows) — Builds a Second Structure
walkthrough.text: Player 1 uses their accumulated resources to build a spy. One wood, one wheat, done. Now the Cows have a house on the board for VP and a spy enabling espionage plays. Player 1 is building a diversified position: territorial control, VP production, and intelligence capability. This is what a winning game looks like in the early rounds.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Chickens) — Builds a House
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has been hoarding resources, and now they cash in. The Chickens build a house on one of their defended territories. One wood, one plutonium, one brick, one wheat. Player 2 now has 1 VP and a spy on the board. Patient resource collection paid off. The key is that Player 2 did not wait too long — they built before the discard phase could force them to throw away good cards.

walkthrough.title: Player 4 (Pigs) — Mistake 3: Ignores the Hand Limit
walkthrough.text: Phase 4 arrives — the Discard phase. Player 4 counts their resource cards. Nine. The hand limit is seven. Player 4 tries to keep all nine, arguing they need every card for their next big play. Nope. The rules are clear: you MUST discard down to seven. Player 4 groans and drops two cards. Those were resources that could have been spent on a house if Player 4 had built during the Actions phase instead of hoarding.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Around Seven Cards
walkthrough.text: The hand limit of seven resources is strict. Plan your building so you spend resources before the discard phase. Wasted resources are wasted turns. If you have eight or nine cards heading into Phase 4, you should have built something during Phases 2 or 3. The best players almost never discard because they spend their resources as fast as they earn them. Every discarded card is a turn you will never get back.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Recap
walkthrough.text: Let's check the scoreboard after Round 1. Player 1 has 1 VP from a house and a spy deployed — solid foundation. Player 2 has 1 VP from a house and a spy, plus a Hacking card in reserve — well protected. Player 3 has 0 VP but strong territorial control and combat momentum — needs to convert land into buildings soon. Player 4 has 0 VP, spent resources on a nuke that scored nothing, lost a territory to Player 3, and had to discard resources. The Pigs are behind, but the game is far from over.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Redemption
walkthrough.text: Round 2 begins and Player 4 has been paying attention. This time, the Pigs play it right. Instead of spreading herds everywhere, Player 4 consolidates on two well-defended territories. Instead of saving up for a nuke, they build a house — one wood, one plutonium, one brick, one wheat. VP on the board at last. They keep their resource hand lean, spending before the discard phase hits. And when Player 3 pushes a herd toward their territory, Player 4 plays a spy card — Propaganda — forcing each opponent to hand over a wheat card. The pig general finally learned: build VP first, manage your hand, and use espionage cards instead of chasing nukes.
walkthrough.image_caption: Pig meeples with a house on the board — VP at last

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations — you have just played through your first round of Farms Race! You have seen all four phases in action, watched Player 4 learn from three common mistakes, and seen how building VP early beats hoarding resources or chasing nukes. Remember: territory without buildings scores nothing, nukes hurt opponents but do not help your score, and the hand limit of seven means you should spend resources before you lose them. Your farm animals are now battle-tested and ready for a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You March
walkthrough.text:
Before you launch into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and the most common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to espionage cards, mutations, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Now go build some houses. Then maybe a nuke or two.

summary.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 4 players: Cows (Yellow), Chickens (Green), Sheep (Gray), Pigs (Pink)
Player 4 (Pigs) makes common mistakes throughout — learn from their errors
Follow along even if you have fewer players

summary.title: The Round — Four Phases
bullets: Every round: Preparation → Actions → Resources → Discard
Phases must be completed in order
Building is allowed during phases 2, 3, or 4

summary.title: Phase 1 — Preparation
bullets: Remove turmoil tokens from your controlled regions
Gain new herd meeples from supply
This is how your faction regenerates between rounds

summary.title: Player 1 (Cows) — Moves and Builds a House
bullets: Moves one herd to expand, builds a house on starting territory
House costs: Wood + Plutonium + Brick + Wheat = 1 VP
Player 1 has points on the board immediately

summary.title: Player 2 (Chickens) — Resource Hoarder with a Plan
bullets: Moves herds defensively, stays on resource-producing territories
Deploys a spy (1 Wood + 1 Wheat) — cheapest build in the game
Spy enables espionage card plays for later

summary.title: Player 3 (Sheep) — Aggressive Expansion
bullets: Pushes herds into territories bordering opponents
Positioning for combat and territory grabs
Valid strategy, but needs buildings to convert territory into VP

summary.title: Player 4 (Pigs) — Mistake 1: Overextends Without Building
bullets: Moves ALL herds outward — controls 5 hexes but builds nothing
Zero VP, zero buildings, herds stretched dangerously thin
Vulnerable to concentrated attacks on any single territory

summary.title: Lesson: Territory Is Just Potential
bullets: Territory without buildings scores zero VP
One house on one territory beats three empty territories
Build early — the scoreboard cares about buildings, not land

summary.title: Player 1 (Cows) — Steady Expansion
bullets: Continues balanced play: expand a little, build a little
Collecting resources while eyeing next building opportunity
This is what a well-paced game looks like

summary.title: Player 2 (Chickens) — Plays a Spy Card Defensively
bullets: Draws Hacking espionage card — blocks an incoming nuke
Holds it in reserve instead of using immediately
Smart defensive play: prepared for nuclear threats

summary.title: Player 3 (Sheep) — Combat and Territory Grab
bullets: Pushes herd into Player 4's thinly defended territory
Combat: roll red dice — Player 3 wins with guns + faction bonus
Player 4 loses a territory due to overextension

summary.title: Player 4 (Pigs) — Mistake 2: Hoards Plutonium for a Nuke
bullets: Saves up for a nuke while other players build houses and score VP
Launches nuke at Player 3 — hurts opponent but scores zero for Player 4
Other players have VP on the board; Player 4 has zero

summary.title: Lesson: Nukes Don't Score You Points
bullets: Nukes hurt opponents but don't add to YOUR scoreboard
Build VP first (houses, barns) before investing in nukes
You can't win with a score of zero no matter how many enemies you destroy

summary.title: Phase 3 — Rolling for Resources
bullets: Each player rolls 2 white resource dice
Collect matching resource cards from supply
Doubles = 2 of that resource; different faces = 1 of each

summary.title: Player 1 (Cows) — Builds a Second Structure
bullets: Builds a spy with accumulated resources
Now has a house (VP) and spy (espionage capability)
Diversified position: territory + VP + intelligence

summary.title: Player 2 (Chickens) — Builds a House
bullets: Patient resource collection pays off — builds a house
Now has 1 VP and a spy on the board
Built before discard phase could force resource loss

summary.title: Player 4 (Pigs) — Mistake 3: Ignores the Hand Limit
bullets: Has 9 resource cards at discard — hand limit is 7
Must discard 2 cards — resources that could have been a house
Should have built during Actions or Resources phase

summary.title: Lesson: Plan Around Seven Cards
bullets: Hand limit of 7 is strict — no exceptions
Spend resources BEFORE the discard phase forces bad choices
Best players almost never discard — they build as fast as they earn
Every discarded card is a wasted turn

summary.title: End of Round Recap
bullets: Player 1: 1 VP (house) + spy — solid foundation
Player 2: 1 VP (house) + spy + Hacking card — well protected
Player 3: 0 VP but strong territory — needs to convert to buildings
Player 4: 0 VP, wasted resources on nuke, lost territory, discarded cards

summary.title: Player 4's Redemption
bullets: Consolidates herds on 2 well-defended territories instead of spreading thin
Builds a house — VP on the board at last
Keeps hand lean, spends before discard phase
Uses Propaganda spy card effectively instead of chasing nukes

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You've practiced all four phases of a round
Watched Player 4 learn from three common mistakes
Don't overextend without building, don't chase nukes over VP, don't ignore the hand limit
Your farm animals are battle-tested and ready for a full game

summary.title: Before You March
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for espionage, mutations, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Build Early, Build Often
walkthrough.text: The single most important lesson in Farms Race: VP wins the game, not territory. A player with two houses on two hexes will beat a player who controls the entire east side of the map but never built anything. Houses cost one wood, one plutonium, one brick, and one wheat. That is four resources for 1 VP. As soon as you have those four cards in hand, build. Do not wait for a better territory or a more strategic moment. The best time to build a house is right now.

walkthrough.title: Spend Before the Discard Phase
walkthrough.text: The hand limit of seven resource cards is not a suggestion — it is a hard rule that catches new players every single game. If you end the Resources phase with more than seven cards, you are throwing away resources. The fix is simple: build something during Phases 2 or 3 before you even roll for resources. Houses, spies, and nukes all cost three or four cards. Spending down to three or four cards before rolling means you almost never hit the cap. Think of Phase 4 as a tax on bad planning.

walkthrough.title: Don't Chase Nukes Early
walkthrough.text: Nukes are the most exciting thing in Farms Race, and that is exactly why they trap new players. A nuke costs one weapon, one brick, and one plutonium — the same brick and plutonium you need for a house. If you spend those resources on a nuke instead of a house, you have traded 1 VP for the ability to hurt someone else. Early in the game, that trade is terrible. Build your own VP first. Nukes become valuable later when you need to stop an opponent from winning or when you are competing for the Nukiest award. But in the first few rounds, build houses.

walkthrough.title: Move Herds to Resource-Producing Territories
walkthrough.text: Not all hexes are created equal. Some territories produce better resources or are in more strategic positions. When you move your herds during the Actions phase, think about what resources you need for your next build. If you need weapons for a barn, position herds on factory hexes. If you need wood and wheat for a spy, head for forests and farmlands. Your herd placement should serve your building plan, not the other way around.

walkthrough.title: Hand Limit Awareness — Plan Around Seven Cards
walkthrough.text: Treat the hand limit as a planning constraint, not a punishment. Before your turn even starts, count your cards. If you have five or six, you can safely roll for resources and still stay under seven. If you already have seven, you MUST build something before rolling, or you will waste whatever you collect. The best players in Farms Race think two phases ahead: they plan their build in Phase 2 based on what they expect to roll in Phase 3. Never enter Phase 4 with more than seven cards.

walkthrough.title: Spy Cards Are More Valuable Than They Look
walkthrough.text: Spies cost just one wood and one wheat — the cheapest build in the game. Many new players skip them because they do not directly score VP. That is a mistake. A single spy enables your entire espionage card hand. Hacking can block a game-ending nuke. Propaganda can strip a key resource from every opponent simultaneously. Double Agent punishes the leader. And the VP espionage cards — Capitalism, Carnivorous, Domestication, Plumbing — are free victory points. Always have at least one spy deployed. Hold a defensive espionage card when opponents have nukes ready.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the beginner traps that lose games. First: overexpanding without building. Five empty territories score zero VP. Second: hoarding plutonium for nukes instead of using it for houses. Plutonium goes into both, but houses score points and nukes do not. Third: ignoring the hand limit and wasting resources to discard. Fourth: neglecting spies — they unlock the best cards in the game. Fifth: retaliating with nukes out of spite instead of building. Revenge feels good but does not move your score. Keep these in mind and you will avoid ninety percent of new player mistakes.

walkthrough.title: Expand Smart, Not Wide
walkthrough.text: Territory control matters in Farms Race, but only if you can defend what you take. Two well-defended hexes with buildings are infinitely more valuable than six hexes spread across the map with one herd each. When you expand, move in pairs — keep herds close enough to support each other in combat. Build a house as soon as you control a new territory. And never leave a building undefended. If an opponent takes a hex with your house on it, that is VP you built that they now threaten to score.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Scoreboard
walkthrough.text: Farms Race ends when someone hits the VP threshold. That means the game can end suddenly, and the player who was quietly building houses while everyone else was fighting might suddenly win. Keep track of everyone's VP. Count their houses, barns, and VP espionage cards. If someone is at the threshold minus one, everything changes — you might need that nuke you have been saving after all. The best defense against a surprise victory is knowing the score at all times.

summary.title: Build Early, Build Often
bullets: VP wins the game, not territory — empty hexes score zero
Build a house as soon as you have Wood + Plutonium + Brick + Wheat
Don't wait for the perfect moment — the best time to build is now

summary.title: Spend Before the Discard Phase
bullets: Hand limit of 7 is strict — discarding means wasted resources
Build during Phases 2 or 3, before rolling for more resources
Spend down to 3-4 cards before rolling to avoid the cap

summary.title: Don't Chase Nukes Early
bullets: Nukes cost the same brick + plutonium that houses need
A nuke trades potential VP for opponent damage — bad early trade
Build VP first, nuke later when stopping a winner or clinching Nukiest

summary.title: Move Herds to Resource-Producing Territories
bullets: Position herds based on what resources you need next
Factories for weapons, forests for wood, farmlands for wheat
Herd placement should serve your building plan

summary.title: Hand Limit Awareness
bullets: Count your cards before your turn starts
5-6 cards = safe to roll; 7 cards = must build first
Plan builds in Phase 2 based on expected Phase 3 rolls
Never enter Phase 4 over the limit

summary.title: Spy Cards Are More Valuable Than They Look
bullets: Cheapest build: 1 Wood + 1 Wheat — unlocks espionage cards
Hacking blocks nukes, Propaganda strips resources from all opponents
VP espionage cards (Capitalism, etc.) are free victory points
Always have at least one spy deployed

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Overexpanding without building — territory without VP is worthless
Hoarding plutonium for nukes instead of houses
Ignoring the hand limit and wasting resources
Neglecting spies — they unlock the game's best cards
Retaliating with nukes out of spite instead of building VP

summary.title: Expand Smart, Not Wide
bullets: Two defended hexes with buildings > six empty hexes
Move herds in pairs for mutual combat support
Build a house immediately when you take new territory
Never leave buildings undefended

summary.title: Watch the Scoreboard
bullets: Game ends at VP threshold — can happen suddenly
Track opponents' houses, barns, and VP espionage cards
If someone is 1 VP from winning, everything changes
Knowing the score at all times is the best defense

walkthrough.title: Mutation Strategies — Mobility and Economy
walkthrough.text: Your mutation card defines your entire strategic identity. Flying is the premier mobility mutation — use it for hit-and-run raids deep into enemy territory. Grab an undefended hex with a building, score the VP, and retreat before they can counterattack. Parasitic rewards proximity to wealthy opponents. Park your herds adjacent to the resource leader and siphon their income every turn. The longer the game goes, the more you steal. Hyper-Intelligent is the pure economy mutation. Extra resource production means you outbuild everyone over time. Focus on houses and barns, ignore combat, and let your production advantage compound. Lucky eliminates resource dice variance entirely — you choose what you collect. Build aggressively since you always have exactly the resources you need.
walkthrough.image_caption: Mutation cards — Flying, Parasitic, Hyper-Intelligent, Lucky

walkthrough.title: Mutation Strategies — Combat and Defense
walkthrough.text: Radioactive makes you immune to nuke damage. This is enormous. Play aggressively near nuke-heavy opponents — their biggest weapon literally does nothing to you. Build barns on contested border hexes and dare them to waste a nuke. Zombie lets you raise defeated herds after combat. Seek constant fights because your losses are recoverable while your opponent's are permanent. Every battle is a war of attrition that you win by default. Regenerative heals your herds mid-combat. Use this to survive battles that should kill you and hold contested territory through pure endurance. Adorable converts enemies into allies — herds that enter your territory defend it instead of attacking. Build high-VP structures and let opponents accidentally donate their troops to your cause.
walkthrough.image_caption: Mutation cards — Radioactive, Zombie, Regenerative, Adorable

walkthrough.title: Mutation Strategies — Chaos and Economy
walkthrough.text: Insecure enables unique trade agreements. Hire another player's resources for just one of yours. This turns diplomacy into a weapon — offer favorable trades to weaker players in exchange for not attacking them. Build alliances through commerce. Deranged makes all your resources wild. This is the ultimate flexibility mutation — any four resource cards become a house, any five become a barn. You never need specific resources, just volume. Build everything as fast as possible. Blue-Blooded gives bonus starting resources, a head start that compounds over the game. Rush a house on turn one while opponents are still collecting their first resources. The earlier you build, the harder you are to catch.
walkthrough.image_caption: Mutation cards — Insecure, Deranged, Blue-Blooded

walkthrough.title: Nuke Timing — When Nuclear Warfare Pays Off
walkthrough.text: Nukes are a late-game weapon. In the early game, the resources spent on a nuke are better invested in a house. But in the late game, three scenarios make nukes correct. First: an opponent is one VP from winning and you need to destroy or contest their building-holding territory. A well-timed nuke places a turmoil token that disrupts their scoring. Second: you can eliminate a cluster of enemy herds and take the territory next turn, capturing their buildings. Third: you need the Nukiest award to reach the VP threshold yourself. The threat of a nuke is often more powerful than using it. A player holding two nuke tokens creates a deterrent zone — nobody wants to push into hexes that might get vaporized. Use that fear strategically.
walkthrough.image_caption: Nuke tokens — their threat value is often greater than their actual use

walkthrough.title: Espionage Card Combos
walkthrough.text: Espionage cards are strongest when sequenced properly. The classic combo is Propaganda followed by Blackmail. Propaganda forces each opponent to give you a named resource — use it to strip a key resource from everyone simultaneously. Then follow up with Blackmail on the leader to steal up to three more of their best cards. In two plays you have gained four to six resources while depleting the table. Double Agent is strongest when you are behind — it steals from everyone ahead of you, making it a catch-up mechanism. Play VP espionage cards — Capitalism, Carnivorous, Domestication, Plumbing — the instant you draw them. There is zero reason to hold a free victory point. Hacking should be held until someone actually threatens a nuke at your territory. Never waste it preemptively.
walkthrough.image_caption: Espionage cards — sequence Propaganda into Blackmail for maximum disruption

walkthrough.title: Territory Chokepoint Control
walkthrough.text: The hex map has natural chokepoints — narrow passages connecting larger clusters of hexes. Controlling these lets you defend large areas with fewer herds. Look for hexes that border three or more other hexes. These are high-value positions. Build a barn on a chokepoint for 2 VP, station multiple herds there, and keep a nuke token in reserve. This creates a fortress that opponents cannot push through without committing their entire force. Chokepoint control also denies opponents access to resource-producing hexes beyond the bottleneck. If you hold the only passage to the wheat fields, everyone who needs wheat has to deal with you first.
walkthrough.image_caption: Hex map — identify and control natural chokepoints between territory clusters

walkthrough.title: Resource Dice Probability
walkthrough.text: Each white resource die has five faces: brick, wheat, wood, weapons, and plutonium. With two dice, you get two resources per roll. The probability of rolling any specific resource on one die is one in five, or twenty percent. The probability of rolling doubles of a specific resource is one in twenty-five, or four percent. The probability of rolling at least one of a specific resource on two dice is thirty-six percent. What does this mean practically? You cannot rely on rolling a specific resource. If you need one more plutonium for a house, you have only a thirty-six percent chance of rolling it. Build with what you have, not what you hope to roll. And if your mutation is Lucky, you skip all this math entirely.

walkthrough.title: Building Order Optimization
walkthrough.text: The age-old question: houses first or straight to barns? Houses cost one of each basic resource plus plutonium and give 1 VP. Barns cost three weapons, one brick, and one plutonium and give 2 VP. Mathematically, a barn gives better VP per build action but requires stockpiling three weapons, which takes longer and risks the hand limit. The optimal opening is usually: build a spy first (cheapest, enables espionage), then build two houses (2 VP, uses diverse resources), then save for a barn (2 VP payoff). Building a spy before anything else is counterintuitive but gives you espionage card access for the rest of the game at minimal cost. Never build a nuke before your first house unless your mutation specifically rewards it.

walkthrough.title: Counter-Strategies for Aggressive Players
walkthrough.text: When an opponent is playing hyper-aggressively — pushing herds into your territory, building nukes, seeking combat — the worst response is to match their aggression. Fighting costs both players herds and resources while the two players NOT fighting build in peace. Instead, use three counter-strategies. First: turtle up. Consolidate herds on your most valuable territories and build barns there. Barns are worth 2 VP and a fortified position is expensive to assault. Second: deploy spies and hold defensive espionage cards. Hacking blocks nukes, Field Hospital heals after battles. Third: expand in the opposite direction. If Player 3 is pushing east, expand west into uncontested territory and build houses there. Let the aggressor earn everyone's hostility while you quietly score points.

walkthrough.title: End-Game VP Calculation and Catch-Up
walkthrough.text: The game ends when someone hits the VP threshold. Late in the game, count everything: houses (1 VP each), barns (2 VP each), award cards (1 VP each), VP espionage cards (1 VP each), and territory bonuses. If you are behind, identify the fastest path to VP. Espionage VP cards are the fastest — draw them and play them immediately for free points. A single house is the next fastest at 1 VP for four resources. The Nukiest award is worth pursuing if you are close to the threshold and already have nukes built. Territory bonuses require map control but can swing close games. If you are ahead, protect what you have. Keep herds on your building territories and hold a defensive nuke or Hacking card. The last thing you want is to lose a barn worth 2 VP in the final round.

summary.title: Mutation Strategies — Mobility and Economy
bullets: Flying: hit-and-run raids, grab undefended VP, retreat
Parasitic: park next to wealthy opponents, siphon resources each turn
Hyper-Intelligent: out-produce everyone, win through economic dominance
Lucky: choose dice results — always have exactly what you need, build aggressively

summary.title: Mutation Strategies — Combat and Defense
bullets: Radioactive: immune to nukes — play aggressively near nuke-heavy opponents
Zombie: seek constant combat — your fallen herds return, opponents' don't
Regenerative: survive battles that should kill you, hold contested territory
Adorable: enemies become allies — build high-VP structures in peace

summary.title: Mutation Strategies — Chaos and Economy
bullets: Insecure: hire opponents' resources for cheap — diplomacy as a weapon
Deranged: all resources are wild — any 4 cards = house, any 5 = barn
Blue-Blooded: bonus starting resources — rush a house on turn 1

summary.title: Nuke Timing — When Nuclear Warfare Pays Off
bullets: Nukes are late-game weapons — early resources are better spent on houses
Nuke to stop a winner, capture enemy buildings, or clinch Nukiest award
Threat value > actual use — holding nuke tokens creates a deterrent zone
Don't nuke out of spite — every launch removes a threat

summary.title: Espionage Card Combos
bullets: Propaganda → Blackmail: strip resources from table, then steal leader's best cards
Double Agent: steal from everyone ahead — best catch-up mechanic
VP cards (Capitalism, etc.): play immediately — zero reason to hold a free VP
Hacking: hold until someone actually threatens a nuke at your territory

summary.title: Territory Chokepoint Control
bullets: Control narrow passages between hex clusters to defend large areas
Build barns (2 VP) on chokepoints for maximum strategic + VP value
Chokepoint + nuke reserve = nearly impregnable fortress
Denies opponents access to resource hexes beyond the bottleneck

summary.title: Resource Dice Probability
bullets: Each die: 20% chance per specific resource (1 in 5 faces)
Doubles of a specific resource: 4% (1 in 25)
At least one specific resource on 2 dice: 36%
Build with what you have, not what you hope to roll

summary.title: Building Order Optimization
bullets: Optimal opening: spy first (cheap, enables espionage) → 2 houses → barn
Houses use diverse resources (easier to collect); barns need 3 weapons (harder)
Never build a nuke before your first house unless mutation rewards it
Spy before house is counterintuitive but gives espionage access all game

summary.title: Counter-Strategies for Aggressive Players
bullets: Don't match aggression — fighting drains both players while others build
Turtle up: consolidate herds, build barns on your best territories
Deploy spies + hold Hacking and Field Hospital for defense
Expand in the opposite direction — build in uncontested territory

summary.title: End-Game VP Calculation and Catch-Up
bullets: Count everything: houses (1), barns (2), awards (1), VP espionage (1), territory bonuses
Behind? Fastest VP: espionage VP cards → house → Nukiest award
Ahead? Protect buildings — keep herds on VP territories, hold defensive cards
Don't lose a 2 VP barn in the final round

entries.definition: One of four playable factions. Led by a bespectacled cow scientist with a rocket launcher. Cows are depicted as the intellectual military-industrial faction. Their color is yellow.
One of four playable factions. Led by a scrappy rooster guerrilla fighter with a bandolier and machete. Chickens are the insurgent rebel faction. Their color is green.
One of four playable factions. Led by a decorated pig military general in a naval uniform, puffing a cigar. Pigs are the establishment military power faction. Their color is pink.
One of four playable factions. Led by a cold-eyed sheep operative carrying a rifle and wearing tactical gear. Sheep are the covert operations faction. Their color is gray.
A building worth 1 victory point. Costs 1 Wood, 1 Plutonium, 1 Brick, and 1 Wheat to build. Can be constructed during phases 2 through 4 of your turn. Must control the territory at game end to score.

A building worth 2 victory points. Costs 3 Weapons, 1 Brick, and 1 Plutonium to build. The most expensive building but the highest VP value. Build on chokepoint territories for maximum strategic value.

A weapon of mass destruction. Costs 1 Weapon, 1 Brick, and 1 Plutonium to build. When built, take a nuke token from the supply. Spend the token to devastate a target region, placing a turmoil token and damaging all herds there. Building the most nukes earns the Nukiest award (1 VP).

A covert agent. Costs 1 Wood and 1 Wheat to build. The cheapest buildable unit. When deployed, place a numbered spy token on the board. Spies enable espionage card plays and special actions. Essential for disrupting opponents.
One of five resource types. Shown on a blue-bordered card with stacked logs. Used in building Houses (1 required) and Spies (1 required). Appears on white resource dice faces as a tree icon.

One of five resource types. Shown on a yellow-bordered card with a sack of grain. Used in building Houses (1 required) and Spies (1 required). Appears on white resource dice faces as a wheat stalk icon.

One of five resource types. Shown on a red-bordered card with a wheelbarrow of bricks. Used in building Houses (1 required), Barns (1 required), and Nukes (1 required). The most versatile resource — needed for three of four building types.

One of five resource types. Shown on a green-bordered card with a mortar launcher. Used in building Barns (3 required) and Nukes (1 required). The primary military resource. Stockpiling weapons signals aggressive intent to opponents.

One of five resource types. Shown on a gray-bordered card with a centrifuge. Used in building Houses (1 required), Barns (1 required), and Nukes (1 required). The nuclear resource — needed for both construction and destruction. Appears on resource dice as a radioactive trefoil icon.
Espionage card. Select an opponent with more VP than you. Look at their resources and steal up to 3 of them of your choice. Powerful for targeted resource denial against the leader.
Espionage card. Use at any time to heal all your herds. Cannot be used to heal herds in a region that has been nuked this turn. Best saved for after a major battle to recover your forces.

Espionage card. Your opponents' citizens have donated to your cause. Name a resource type. Each player who has any of that resource must give you one. Scales with player count — better in 4-player games.
Espionage card. Steal 2 random resources from each player who has more VP than you. Powerful catch-up mechanic that hits multiple opponents simultaneously. Best when you are behind.

Espionage card. When someone tries to nuke you, you may play this to take no damage. Your agents sabotage an incoming nuke. The ultimate defensive card — hold it when opponents have nuke tokens ready.
Espionage card. Take 3 of one kind of resource from the Supply. Does not steal from opponents — takes directly from the shared supply. Good for completing a specific building cost quickly.

Espionage card. Deal 2 damage to an adjacent region. You may use this to nuke other players back when nuked. Does not collect a Nuke Token and does not count toward the Nukiest award. A retaliatory strike option.
Espionage card worth 1 VP. You leave socialism for its evil twin! A scoring espionage card — play it immediately for the free victory point. Shows a pig in a business suit fleeing with bags of money.
Espionage card worth 1 VP. Your agents have discovered the incomparable taste of meat! A scoring espionage card — play immediately for the free VP. Shows a chicken as a chef with a roasted turkey.
Espionage card worth 1 VP. Your agents have tamed your former masters! A scoring espionage card — play immediately. Shows a cow with a domesticated human farmer on a leash.
Espionage card worth 1 VP. Your agents discover plumbing. Troop morale is at an all-time high! A scoring espionage card — play immediately. Shows a happy pig with indoor plumbing.
Mutation card. Your herds can move to any region on the map as an extra movement action. Enables hit-and-run raids and deep strikes into enemy territory. Best for aggressive expansion strategies.
Mutation card. Steal resources from opponents in adjacent regions. Passive income that triggers when enemies are nearby. Rewards positioning herds next to resource-rich opponents.
Mutation card. Gain extra resources during the resource phase. Your genius farm animals have optimized production. Enables faster building and economic dominance over time.
Mutation card. You do not take damage from nukes. Your mutated herds thrive in nuclear fallout. Completely negates the nuke threat, allowing aggressive play near nuke-heavy opponents.

Mutation card. Do not kill enemy herders — they stay and defend your region. You control all herders in your region. Gain a Herd Token for each region you control. Converts enemies into allies through sheer cuteness.
Mutation card. If your herds would be defeated in combat, you may choose to raise them back. Your casualties return as new herds. Rewards constant combat since your losses are recoverable.
Mutation card. Overreact to all situations. You may hire the resources and collections of another player in a trade agreement for 1 of your resources. Enables unique trading and resource conversion.
Mutation card. All your resources are wild. Say something crazy to your neighbors about your plans. Chaotic resource conversion and psychological warfare combined.
Mutation card. Once per combat, you may heal all of your herds. If all your herds are at full strength, you may heal herds of allies in the combat. Excellent for sustained warfare.
Mutation card. Instead of rolling resource dice, just declare what resource you want. Everyone worships your super-duper lucky farm animals. Guarantees optimal resource income every turn.
Mutation card. Start with an extra bonus resource and an honorary title instead of a standard start. Gives an economic head start that compounds over the game. Best for fast-building strategies.

Your animal meeples on the board. Herds can move to adjacent hexes or be healed during the Actions phase. They fight in combat, can be damaged or destroyed, and are gained during the Preparation phase.
A single hex tile on the map. Territories produce resources, hold buildings, and are the key to victory. Control a territory by having herds on it with no enemy herds present.

A round token with a burning building icon placed on regions that have been nuked or devastated. Turmoil tokens are removed during the controlling player's Preparation phase. Regions with turmoil cannot produce normally.

A round token with a mushroom cloud icon. Gained when you build a nuke (costs Weapons + Brick + Plutonium). Spend to devastate a target region. Having nuke tokens creates a deterrent effect on opponents.
A round token with a sheriff star badge icon. Used to track victory points earned from buildings and award cards. Houses earn 1 VP, barns earn 2 VP, and award cards earn 1 VP each.
Two white dice with resource icons on each face: brick, wheat, wood, weapons, and plutonium. Rolled during Phase 3 (Resources) to determine which resource cards you collect from the supply.
Six red dice with combat icons: guns for direct damage, spy silhouettes in fedora hats for espionage effects, and animal heads for faction-specific abilities. Rolled when herds engage in combat.
Small round tokens numbered 1, 2, 3 plus a crosshair icon. Each player gets a set in their faction color. Placed on the board when building a spy. Enable espionage card plays and covert actions.
Award card worth 1 VP. Goes to the player who has built the most nukes over the course of the game. Shows a chicken riding a nuclear missile. Encourages arms-race behavior and nuclear proliferation.
Phase 1 of each turn. Remove turmoil tokens from your controlled regions and gain new herd meeples from your supply. This is how your faction recovers and rebuilds between turns.
Phase 2 of each turn. Move or heal each of your herds. Moving into enemy-occupied hexes triggers combat. Building is allowed during this phase.
Phase 3 of each turn. Roll the two white resource dice and collect matching resource cards from the supply. Building is allowed during this phase.
Phase 4 of each turn. If you have more than 7 resource cards, discard down to 7. Choose which to discard. Building is allowed during this phase before discarding.
